raw_recent_text: "Ugh, not again," you spout as the old wagon shutters, then dies.
Stranded on the road again, but this time, your money has gone dry.
All but your 50 dollar bill that Grandma gave you on her last Christmas.
You've been saving it for when you needed it most, but surely, it won't be enough.
The door creaks open, and you step out into the night sky, coughing up the smoke from your fried vehicle.
After pushing your car off the road and between the trees, there isn't much else left for you to do,
so you begin to wander down the dark, lonely street.
But at the end of the road, where concrete turned to stone turned to dirt, you notice a light up ahead, on the top of a hill.
As you waltz into the old, wooden shack, your eyes begin to light up with the fire of a thousand suns.
Roulette wheels! Poker tables! And in a dark corner of the abandoned casino, sits a dealer, shuffling cards for a new round of Blackjack.
That 50 dollars might just come in handy after all. Thanks, Grandma!
As you go to sit down at the table, you hear the Dealer cough, then watch as he sits up.
In a deep, and yet strained voice, the Dealer, cloaked in darkness, poses a question to you.
Would you like to play a game of Blackjack?
You have $50

You've survived 1 day!
You started your journey with just $50.
Since then, you've managed to lose $12.
That brings you to a grand total of $38!
Hey, at least you can afford... some things. Not many things. But some.
Life's a garden, dig it. You're doing just that, and it's admirable.
For those who enjoy a bit of bathroom philosophy:

"Grief, I've learned, is really just love. It's all the love you want to give, but cannot. All that unspent love gathers up in the corners of your eyes, the lump in your throat, and in that hollow part of your chest. Grief is just love with no place to go."
Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 2 ~ ~ ~  True
You slept so hard you forgot where you were for a second. That's the good kind of confusion.
The temperature is perfect. Not too hot, not too cold. The car seat cradles you like it was designed for sleeping.
It wasn't. But tonight, everything conspires to give you rest. The parking lot is quiet. No sirens. No drunks. No existential dread.
You sleep like a baby. A baby who lives in a car, but a well-rested baby nonetheless.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
The green felt remembers you. So do I.

The man reaches your window, and in a deep southern accent, he begins to talk to you.
"Howdy, partner! The name's Jameson. Davey Jameson.
"I happen to notice you were admiring my steed. He's a beauty, isn't he. "
"You see, it's common courtesy when a cowboy rides by, "
to give their mighty steed a carrot, as a way to express your gratitude for their hardwork and commitment to the job."
"You my friend, you are carrotless. That's quite a disrespectful showing towards my steed. "
"My, my, this can't do at all. What if another cowboy comes by, you're just gonna disrespect their steed, too? "
"Tell you what, I happen to have one spare carrot in my pouch. Take this, and be ready. "
"You never know when a cowboy's gonna come trotting by."
Davey Jameson hands you his Carrot, and smiles.
"See, with this carrot in your possession, you're ready for anytime a cowboy strolls on down this road. "
"Just give their steed a carrot, and they'll be very grateful."
And with that, Jameson reins his horse high into the air, gives you a yee-haw, then dashes off down the road.
When you turn the key, there's a horrible grinding noise from under the hood.
The starter motor is dying. Every start could be its last.
Starter motors cost around $200-400 to replace.
For now, it still works. Barely. Loudly. Worryingly.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Another night. Another dance with chance.

You're in the 'ramen noodle' tax bracket.
Do you think maybe you could be a bit better at Blackjack? Just think about it.
If aliens could talk, they'd probably say:
"Why ponder the meaning of life when you can dance with the whimsical ants under the moonlit spaghetti? It's all about finding the rhythm in the chaos of cereal whispers."
Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 4 ~ ~ ~  True
You wake up refreshed and optimistic. The optimism will probably wear off by noon, but for now, you bask in it.
You prayed for a miracle. Got a parking ticket instead.
You crumple the ticket up. What are they going to do, take your car? You live in it. That's cruel and unusual punishment, probably.
You look up at the sky. "Very funny." The sky says nothing. As usual.
You lean against the hood of the wagon and look up. You find Orion's Belt. The Big Dipper. That's... all you know.
But it doesn't matter. The names don't matter. What matters is that something enormous and ancient is looking back at you.
And it doesn't care about your problems. Somehow, that's comforting.
Still, it's beautiful. You feel small, but in a comforting way.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Ready to play? The question is always yes, isn't it?

Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 5 ~ ~ ~  True
Your body feels like it got a factory reset overnight. Joints? Smooth. Back? Fine. Mood? Immaculate.
You drive your car to a gas station for supplies. Just snacks. Maybe some coffee.
You're browsing the chips when the door slams open.
"EVERYBODY ON THE GROUND! NOW!"
A man with a shotgun. Ski mask. Shaking hands. This is really happening.
The cashier freezes. A mother clutches her child. An old man drops his coffee.
The robber swings the gun around, wild-eyed.
You're browsing the chips when the door slams open.
"EVERYBODY ON THE GROUND! NOW!"
A man with a shotgun. Ski mask. Shaking hands. This is really happening.
The cashier freezes. A mother clutches her child. An old man drops his coffee.
The robber swings the gun around, wild-eyed.
You duck behind a shelf. Slowly, carefully, you crawl toward the back.
The robber is focused on the register. You're almost to the storage room...
You make it. Hide behind boxes of toilet paper and canned goods.
You can hear everything. The shouting. The crying. The crash of the register hitting the floor.
Then sirens. Running footsteps. Silence.
The police find you an hour later, still hiding. Curled up like a child.
Your mind feels... foggy. Something isn't right.
After spending an hour sitting in your car doing nothing, you feel like you should get some exercise.
You get out of the wagon, and begin to jog down the road.
A couple hours go by, and while jogging back, you see the wagon in the distance.
You get back to the car, and get in, out of breath from your trip. You start the wagon and run the AC, and you feel good inside.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
You feel anxious tonight.
Shuffle. Deal. Win. Lose. Repeat. Shall we?

Your current sanity: 70%
You wake up refreshed and optimistic. The optimism will probably wear off by noon, but for now, you bask in it.
You're looking for a dropped quarter under the driver's seat when your hand
touches something cold and hard. Metal.
You pull it out. It's a lockbox. A real, proper lockbox. Steel. Heavy. About the size
of a shoebox. There's a keyhole on the front and a combination dial on top.
It's padlocked shut with a serious-looking Master lock.
You shake it. Something slides around inside. Multiple somethings.
They clink against each other like glass or metal.
This wasn't here yesterday. You'd have noticed. Which means someone put it
in your car while you were sleeping.
On the bottom, scratched into the metal with something sharp:
"DON'T OPEN UNTIL READY"
You count your money three times. You get a different number each time.
As you're walking along the side of the road, something catches your eye. A flash of white, darting between the bushes. A rabbit!
Without thinking, you give chase. The little creature bounds ahead of you, zigging and zagging through the underbrush with seemingly effortless grace.
You run and run, but no matter how fast you go, the rabbit stays just out of reach. Its white tail bobs mockingly in the moonlight.
Finally, you stop, hands on your knees, gasping for breath. When you look up, the rabbit is gone, vanished into the night like it was never there.
You trudge back to your wagon, defeated. But something tells you this isn't the last time you'll see that rabbit.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
You feel anxious tonight.
Back for more? The cards appreciate your persistence.

A scraggly stray cat jumps onto your hood and stares at you through the windshield.
It meows. Loudly. Insistently. Like it's judging your entire existence.
You share your meager rations. The cat purrs and curls up on your hood.
It's still there when you wake up the next morning. You have a friend now.
You decide to call it Whiskers.
You have befriended Whiskers!
Whiskers paws at your face until you open your eyes. Then she walks away. She just wanted to know you were alive.
Having Whiskers by your side makes the world a little less cruel.
In the nightmare, the cards are blank. Every single one. You flip them over and they're just... white.
The Dealer keeps dealing blank cards. You keep losing anyway. "Hit or stand?" he asks.
You scream. No sound comes out. The Dealer deals another blank card. You bust.
You jolt awake. Your mouth tastes like copper. Your heart is trying to leave your chest.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
You feel anxious tonight.

You step out of your car and a group of teenagers blocks your path. Their leader steps forward.
"Yo! This is our turf. You wanna pass? You gotta DANCE."
A boombox appears. Music starts playing. The crowd forms a circle.
It's dance battle time. What's your move?
You glide backwards like you're on ice. Smooth. Real smooth.
You WIN the dance battle! The gang cheers!
They shower you with singles. You collect $69!
Your new balance is $26 + $69 = $95
Whiskers yawns, showing every single tiny fang. Good morning to you too.
Having Whiskers by your side makes the world a little less cruel.
You dream of winning. The moment the million hits your account.
Confetti. Champagne. People cheering your name.
The Dealer shakes your hand. His grip is warm. He says, "I always knew you'd make it."
You wake up smiling. Then you check your balance. Still not there.
But soon. Soon.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
You've got that look in your eye. Hope. Or madness. Sometimes they're the same.

Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 12 ~ ~ ~  True
A bird is singing outside your car. You don't want to punch it. Progress.
You're leaning against your car, wincing. Everything hurts. Your body has been through
more punishment than it was designed for.
A man appears from behind the gas station. He's wearing a wrinkled white coat over a Hawaiian shirt.
He has a doctor's bag — the old-fashioned kind, black leather — and a smile that belongs
on a used car salesman.
"You look like a person in pain. I'm a person who fixes pain."
He opens his bag. Inside are rows and rows of pill bottles, neatly organized.
"Name's Dr. Feelgood. Not my real name, obviously. But nobody's using their real name ""out here, are they?"
He holds up a single white pill between his thumb and forefinger.
"First one's free. That's not a gimmick. I genuinely want to help. ""Think of it as a... demonstration."
He shrugs and walks away, humming. The bag full of pills clinking softly.
The pain is still there. It's always there.
Something's definitely wrong with your body. That's for certain.
Whiskers meows at the door. She wants out. The thought of her leaving makes your chest hurt.
After wandering from your vehicle, you find yourself deep in the woods.
Squirrels run by and up into the trees. The sun hits every branch and casts a shadow below.
And you wander on a natural path, journeying into the unknown.
You walk, and walk, and walk further down the path, before the forest opens up to the main road.
You follow the road back to your wagon, wondering if there was anything you missed.
At least you made it back safe and sound.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Each night you return. Each night, the cards wait.

You scream but water fills your lungs instead of air.
You wake up GASPING. Clawing at your throat. Soaking wet with sweat.
Just a dream. Just a dream. Just a—
Your feet are wet. You look down.
Rain. It's raining. Water leaked through a crack in your window.
Just rain. You're fine. You're fine. You're fine.

You stay at the railing. Minutes pass. Maybe hours.
A car stops. Someone gets out. A woman's voice, soft and scared.
"Hey. Hey there. Are you okay? Please step back from the edge."
You look at her. She looks terrified. Terrified for YOU.
When's the last time anyone was scared for you? When's the last time anyone cared?

You wake up and actually want to get out of the car. This is a historic moment.
The Dealer shuffles the deck. A card slips out — but it's not a playing card.
It's a photograph. Old. Faded. Wallet-sized.
The Dealer snatches it up fast, but you've already seen it: a woman and a little girl
standing in front of a yellow house. The woman is laughing. The girl is blowing out candles
on a birthday cake.
The Dealer tucks the photo into his vest pocket without a word. His hands,
always perfectly steady, have the faintest tremor.

always perfectly steady, have the faintest tremor.
The Dealer stops shuffling. He looks at you with those dark eyes.
For a moment, he's not the Dealer. He's just a man with a memory that hurts.
"Some cards aren't meant to be played. Let's leave it at that."
He resumes shuffling. The moment is over. But the tremor isn't.
For the rest of the session, the Dealer plays differently. Quieter.
Almost gentle with the cards.
You have a seriously high fever, and feel like puking.
Something's definitely wrong with your body. That's for certain.
You can't feel your heartbeat anymore. You check. It's still there. You think.
Bored out of your mind, you decide to wander along the side of the road, just to get a change of scenery from the dusty leather seats of your wagon.
As you take step after step over the asphalt, you notice a ditched wallet, just laying there. I guess it's yours now.
Inside the wallet, you find $27 dollars.

raw_recent_text: You slept so hard you forgot where you were for a second. That's the good kind of confusion.
You finally open the lockbox. Inside: $2,000 in cash, a set of house keys,
a sealed letter, and a photograph of you asleep in your car.
Your new balance is $59 + $2,000 = $2,059
You open the letter. The handwriting is the same as the notes —
neat, deliberate, careful.
"Dear stranger,"
"I know you don't know who I am. That's okay. You don't need to."

"I was where you are three years ago. Same parking lot. Same car seat. Same view of the same ceiling. I played cards too — not blackjack, poker — but the principle is the same. Gambling to survive. Surviving to gamble."
"Someone helped me. I never found out who. They left me money and a chance and a note that said 'Pass it on.'"
"So I'm passing it on."
"The keys are to an apartment. Studio, nothing fancy. Rent is paid through the end of the month. After that, it's up to you. The address is on the keychain."
"I know what you're thinking. Is this real? Is this a trap? Who does this?"
"People who remember."
"The photograph was just to prove I could have hurt you. I didn't. I won't. I'm just someone who was you, once."
"Good luck. And when you can — pass it on."
It's unsigned.
You sit in your car holding a letter from a stranger who was you three years ago,
and you cry. Not because you're sad. Because someone remembered what it felt like
and did something about it.
with hot water. A window that faces east — morning sun.
On the kitchen counter, one more note:
"The fridge has groceries. The bed has clean sheets. Welcome home — for now."
It's the nicest room you've been in since everything went wrong.
You sit on the bed. You take off your shoes. You look at the ceiling,
which is not the ceiling of your car.
It's different up here.
A sense of clarity washes over you. The fog lifts, if only a little.
You fold the letter and put it in your pocket. Maybe one day you'll pass it on too.
You feel much less sick than you did yesterday, which is always good.
Something's definitely wrong with your body. That's for certain.
After wandering from your vehicle, you find yourself deep in the woods.
Deer dart by you. Trees branches sway back and forth.
And you wander along a river, journeying into the unknown.
You keep walking, and keep walking, and keep walking, and eventually, the woods clear up, and you're back on the main road.
You follow it back to your car, wondering if there was anything else to see. Well, at least you're home, safe and sound.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
You feel anxious tonight.
Take your seat. The wheel of fortune turns for no one.

The lead duck - wearing what appears to be a tiny top hat - stops in front of you and quacks authoritatively.

The parade leads you through alleys, across a parking lot, and finally to... a fountain full of coins.
The top hat duck looks at you, then at the fountain, then quacks approvingly.
You fish out $25 in loose change. The ducks quack in celebration.

you roll down the window just a crack.
"nice pile of cash you got there," he rasps, nodding at the money in your passenger seat.
"you look like a collector. a finder of rare things."
he glances around nervously, then leans closer.

"if you ever find yourself with... unusual items... things that don't belong in the light of day... i can make them disappear. for a fair price."
he taps his nose conspiratorially.
"collectibles, trinkets, treasures. things you picked up on your... adventures. i don't ask questions. just cash on the barrel."
he hands you a grimy business card through the crack in the window. it reads: "grimy gus's pawn emporium - we buy what others won't"
"come by sometime. you won't regret it. probably."
he shuffles off into the morning mist before you can respond.

you wake up with energy. like, real energy. not 'i didn't die in my sleep' energy.
a glossy black limousine is quietly approaching your wagon.
as you sit up from your slumber, you read 'oswald's optimal outoparts' cursively engraved in gold letters on the side of the limo.
waving the vehicle down, the limo slows, then stops before you.
the door opens vertically, and a large red carpet is rolled out onto the street.
you watch in awe as a man, with a combover and a tuxedo, walks out before you. he coughs, then speaks.
"why hello there! the name's oswald, as you can see by my nametag.
do you like my bowtie? well of course you do! it appears your limousine has broken down."
oswald pulls a gold whistle out of his pocket, and blows into it deeply.
"oh stuart!" you watch as a bald man in a tailcoat suit, no taller than 4 feet, hobbles over to oswald's side.
"this is stuart! he will fix your limousine up for a fair price.
let's say, i don't know, i suppose a fair price is 500,000 dollars.
okay, the look on your face says that i'm making a big mistake.
let's try $850, and stuart here will get you back on the road! do you accept?"
"really? you don't want my services? i'm so sorry stuart, but it appears they don't want our services."
stuart begins to break down into tears, and he runs quickly back into the limo.
"shame on you! shame on you! i hope to never see the likes of you again."
you watch as oswald rolls up the red carpet, gets back in the limo, and drives off into the distance.
something's definitely wrong with your body. that's for certain.
